# Conceptual Overview

### Website Overview

#### What is the My Favorite Albums site?

My Favorite Albums is an interactive website that lets you explore and analyze a personal music collection. The site organizes album rankings, ratings, and listening preferences into an easy-to-navigate dashboard with seven different tabs, each offering unique insights into the music data. 

### Understanding the Technology

**GitHub**: GitHub is an online service that stores and organizes software in the cloud and is especially useful for sharing or collaborating on software projects. *GitHub is where the starter code can be found for My Favorite Albums to enable you, the developer, to customize and publish the site.*

**RStudio:** RStudio is a popular IDE (integrated development environment) of choice for interacting with and using R code. *RStudio is how you, the developer, will test your web app before publishing it, and how you will publish it using Shiny Apps.*

- R version 4.5+ recommended  
- RStudio 2024+ recommended

**Google Sheets:** Google Sheets is an online software, part of the Google Workspace, that allows users to create and edit CSV files. 

**Shiny:** Shiny is an R package that enables developers to create and publish web applications entirely using R. 

**Shiny Apps:** Shiny Apps is a web service that allows developers to publish and host web applications that use the Shiny package. To publish your customized My Favorite Albums web app, you will need to create a free account at [shinyapps.io](http://shinyapps.io). This will allow you to deploy and host your web app on the Shiny Apps servers so anyone with the link can access it through their web browser.

![][image1]  
The above image shows the general development process and interactions

### Understanding the Code

#### Infrastructure files

The infrastructure files connect the various code files to serve the web app and key functionalities. These are the files that interact with Shiny Apps to define the user interface and coordinate data analysis and retrieval from the analysis files. These key files include app.R, app\_server.R, and app\_ui.R.

#### Analysis files

The analysis files are the files of R code that are responsible for querying and analyzing the data. Each tab of the web app answers one question or allows users to investigate trends in listening history, and these are the files that support those queries. The files that support data analysis for the web app are: albums\_by\_year.r, albums\_by\_band.r, compare\_bands.r, fav\_bands.r, home.r, number\_one\_albums.r, and vinyl.r.

### Key Terms

- Web App/Application: A piece of software that exists online and can be accessed through a web browser.   
- User Interface: The graphical representation of the web app that a user can interact with in their browser.  
- Package or Library: A trusted collection of software that can be downloaded and used in code. *Packages can be referenced and used, so the developer doesn’t have to write any of the code themselves.*  

### Understanding the Data

The web app uses a CSV file with information about albums, artists, and supplemental information. To customize the web app, custom data that matches the existing data fields and formatting restrictions needs to be created and named to match the data references in the code.

#### Customizing the data

**Note:** No customization of the code is required to publish the web app with custom data.   
The code refers to the *album-rankings.csv* file when creating and interacting with the web app, so as long as the custom CSV file is named and formatted appropriately and located in the correct folder (*/data)* at the time of publishing, the published web app will use the custom data. You will create this table with your own data in Google Sheets.

#### 

#### Data dictionary 
The web app relies on data imported as a CSV file. The data dictionary, data constraints, and key distinctions can be found in the [reference documentation page](https://aidanbartlett0.github.io/ENGL396final/#/Reference_Documentation_dev?id=data-dictionary).

### Scope

The software allows users to interact with the published web application through their browser, using dropdown menus, sliders, and buttons to explore the music data without any coding or technical setup.

![][image2]

The above image shows the software UI with the data from the *My Favorite Albums* starter code

To customize the software with your own data and publish it to the web, you will need to slightly edit some hard-coded values for date ranges and sliders to match the UI with your data. Creating a custom dataset of albums is the only hands-on requirement, aside from the technical setup of R, RStudio, GitHub, and Shiny Apps.
